title: Al-Razi (Rhazes)
summary: The Persian physician of ninth- and tenth-century Rayy and Baghdad, Latinized as Rhazes, who ran hospitals, kept careful records of his patients, and wrote one of the earliest clear clinical accounts distinguishing smallpox from measles. His vast medical compendium, the Hawi or Continens, carried his case-based, observational style into the European universities, while his willingness to test Galen against his own findings made him one of the medieval world's sharpest clinical empiricists — and attacks on prophecy attributed to him by hostile opponents made him one of its most controversial figures.
hook: When a patient came to him with fever and a rash, al-Razi did not reach first for a textbook. He watched. He noted the color of the eruption, the timing of the fever, the look of the patient's eyes, and he wrote it all down. Out of that habit came the first medical text that told smallpox and measles apart by their signs at the bedside, not by theory. He directed hospitals, kept running records of hard cases, and where his own eyes contradicted the great Greek authority Galen, he trusted his eyes. The Latin West knew him as Rhazes and studied him for centuries. His own world also remembered him as a man who, in works his enemies quoted back at him, questioned whether any prophet spoke for God.
associatedWorks: Kitab al-Hawi fi al-Tibb (The Comprehensive Book / Continens)
Kitab al-Judari wa al-Hasba (On Smallpox and Measles)

beginner.theFigure:
A doctor in Persia around the year 900 sat at the bedside of a sick patient and wrote down what he saw. He noted the fever, the rash, the look of the skin, and how the illness changed from day to day. That doctor was al-Razi. In Europe he was later called Rhazes. He ran hospitals in the city of Rayy and in Baghdad. He treated rich patients and poor ones. And he kept careful records of what worked and what did not.

Al-Razi believed that watching the patient mattered more than repeating what old books said. This sounds obvious today. It was not obvious then. Most medicine of his time leaned heavily on the ancient Greek doctors, above all a man named Galen. Al-Razi respected Galen. But when his own observations did not match Galen's claims, he said so. He wrote a whole book pointing out where the great authority seemed wrong.

He was also an alchemist, an early kind of chemist. He worked in a laboratory with real equipment, heating and mixing substances and sorting them into groups. By the time he died around 925, he had written a huge number of books, mostly about medicine.
beginner.worldOfIdeas:
Al-Razi lived during a great age of learning in the Islamic world. For two centuries, scholars in Baghdad had been translating the books of ancient Greece into Arabic. Works on medicine, mathematics, and philosophy were now available to read and argue over. A doctor like al-Razi could study the Greek master Galen and the philosopher Aristotle in his own language.

Many doctors treated these old books as the indispensable starting point, and some treated them as nearly final. The Greek authorities had figured out medicine, the thinking went, and the job now was to learn and apply it. Al-Razi did not accept this. He thought medicine should grow through new observation. A doctor in the present could see things the ancients had missed.

This was a bold attitude. It did not mean throwing out the past. Al-Razi used Greek medicine every day. But he treated it as a starting point, not a finished answer. He trusted careful looking, and he was willing to record a case that did not fit the rules.
beginner.centralIdea:
Al-Razi's main idea was simple but powerful: watch the patient closely, and let what you see guide you. His most famous proof of this was a short book on two diseases, smallpox and measles. Both bring fever and a rash. Earlier writers had treated them as almost the same thing. Al-Razi looked harder. He described how each one started, how the rash appeared, and how the illness changed over days. By doing this, he told them apart at the bedside.

He kept this habit with all his patients. He wrote down hard cases like notes in a journal. He recorded what he tried and what happened next, even when a treatment failed. Over time these notes piled up into a kind of evidence. He was building medicine out of real cases, not just rules from old books.

This way of working took courage. Trusting your own eyes over a famous authority could look like pride to other doctors. But al-Razi thought it was the honest path. The sick person in front of him was the real test.
beginner.howSpread:
Al-Razi's medical books traveled to Europe through translation. In Spain and other places where cultures met, scholars turned Arabic books into Latin. His huge medical encyclopedia, the Hawi, became known in Latin as the Continens. European doctors valued it for the sheer number of cases and authorities packed inside. His shorter handbook served as a teaching text for students.

In the Islamic world he was admired as a doctor and widely quoted. But a later physician, Ibn Sina, wrote a grander and more orderly medical system. Over time Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine became the main book used to teach students, and al-Razi's looser, case-based writing was used less in the classroom.

His ideas about religion spread in a stranger way. The books where he is said to have attacked prophecy mostly did not survive. We know them mainly through writers who quoted him in order to argue against him. So his boldest claims reached later readers already wrapped in attack.
beginner.contestedInheritance:
Al-Razi is remembered in two very different ways. As a doctor, his fame is secure. His careful method and his book on smallpox and measles make him one of the great observers of his age. Few people argue about this part of his name.

The philosopher is harder to judge. Al-Razi is reported to have held daring views: that reason, not religion, leads to truth, and that prophets were not specially chosen by God. These ideas come down to us mostly through people who wanted to prove him wrong. How fairly they described his real position is hard to know. Some of it may be twisted or made to sound worse than it was.

So he means different things to different readers. To some he is a hero of free thinking, a man who questioned dogma a thousand years ago. To others that picture rests on too little solid proof. What is clear is narrower. He valued reason highly. He questioned authority openly. And that mix made people uneasy then, and keeps his memory in dispute now.

general.theFigure:
There is a famous story about al-Razi and a Baghdad hospital around the year 900. To choose the site for a new infirmary, he is said to have hung pieces of meat around the city and watched where they rotted slowest. The tale may be later legend. But it captures the man, a physician who trusted what he could observe over what he could merely assume. He directed hospitals in his native Rayy, near modern Tehran, and in Baghdad, the great city of the Abbasid Caliphate.

Al-Razi was a clinician first. He kept detailed notes on individual patients, recording symptoms, the course of an illness, and the results of treatment. From these records came his most famous medical achievement: a short treatise distinguishing smallpox from measles by their clinical signs. Earlier writers had blurred the two diseases together. Al-Razi separated them through observation at the bedside, describing how each looked and progressed. The work was translated into Latin and other languages and remained a reference for centuries.

His largest work was the Hawi, a vast compendium assembled partly from his own case notes and partly from the Greek, Indian, and Arabic sources he had read. Latin Europe knew it as the Continens. Alongside it stood the Kitab al-Mansuri, a more compact medical handbook that European students read as the Liber ad Almansorem.
general.worldOfIdeas:
Al-Razi worked at the height of what scholars call the Islamic Golden Age, in the wake of a long movement that had translated Greek learning into Arabic. The medicine of his day was built on Galen, the second-century Greek physician whose system explained illness through a balance of bodily fluids, or humors. To be a serious doctor meant mastering Galen. Most practitioners treated his texts as close to complete.

Al-Razi broke with this reverence in a pointed way. In a work titled Doubts about Galen, he laid out specific places where his own clinical experience contradicted the master. He did not reject Galen wholesale, and he shared much of the humoral framework. But he insisted that observation could correct even the greatest authority. This was an unusual stance in a tradition that prized the careful transmission of inherited knowledge.

He was also a working alchemist. Where much alchemy of the period was wrapped in mystical secrecy, al-Razi wrote practically, describing apparatus, procedures, and a classification of substances. For this many historians count him among the figures who helped push chemistry toward an experimental craft, distinct from its mystical roots. The line between the two was far from clean in his own practice.
general.centralIdea:
Al-Razi's central conviction was that medicine advances through observation rather than authority alone. His treatise on smallpox and measles is the clearest demonstration. He did not begin by asking what Galen had said about fevers and eruptions. He described what he had actually seen: the timing, the look of the rash, the signs that told one disease from the other. He treated the patient in front of him as the primary evidence.

This empiricism extended to how he gathered knowledge. The case records behind the Hawi read almost like a notebook, preserving particular patients, their symptoms, and what happened when he tried a given remedy. He was building medicine from the ground up, from accumulated cases, not only from the top down through doctrine.

His attitude carried risk. Questioning Galen could look like arrogance to colleagues who saw the Greek corpus as settled. And al-Razi pressed his skepticism further than medicine. In philosophical works, he argued that human reason was the proper guide to truth, and that established authority should not be accepted simply because it was old or revered. That same instinct, applied to religion, would make him a deeply controversial figure.
general.howSpread:
Al-Razi's medical works traveled west through the same channel that carried so much Arabic science to Europe: translation. In twelfth- and thirteenth-century centers such as Toledo, scholars rendered his books into Latin. The Continens, the Latin Hawi, became a heavyweight reference in European medical libraries, valued for the sheer mass of cases and authorities it gathered. The Liber ad Almansorem served as a teaching text, and one of its sections circulated for generations as a standard short manual.

In the Islamic world his reputation was high but not unmixed. As a clinician he was admired and widely cited. Later physicians, above all Ibn Sina, produced grander, more philosophically ordered systems. Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine eventually overshadowed al-Razi's looser, case-based work in much formal teaching, though al-Razi's clinical writings kept their authority.

His philosophical and religious reputation followed a darker path. The works in which he is reported to have attacked prophecy and organized religion largely do not survive intact. We know them mostly through hostile summaries written by opponents, including Ismaili theologians who set out to refute him. As a result his most radical ideas reached later readers already framed as dangerous error.
general.contestedInheritance:
Al-Razi's legacy splits along the line between the physician and the philosopher. As a doctor he is secure. His clinical method, his case records, and his account of smallpox and measles mark him as one of the finest observers of the premodern world. His texts shaped European medicine for centuries. Few dispute this part of his standing.

The philosopher is harder to assess, because the evidence is thin and partisan. Al-Razi is reported to have held strikingly heterodox views: that reason, not revelation, leads to truth; that prophets were not uniquely chosen by God; that organized religion could mislead. These claims come down to us mostly through writers who quoted him in order to condemn him. How accurately they captured his actual position is genuinely uncertain. Some of what is attributed to him may be exaggerated or distorted by the framing of his enemies.

This makes him a contested inheritance in more than one sense. For some modern readers he is a hero of free thought, a rationalist who challenged dogma a thousand years ago. For others that picture rests on too little reliable text. What can be said with confidence is narrower. He prized reason highly. He questioned authority in medicine openly, and in religion at least in part. That combination unsettled his contemporaries, and it complicates his memory still.

advanced.theFigure:
The deepest interpretive problem in al-Razi scholarship is the gap between the physician we can read and the philosopher we mostly cannot. His major medical works survive, many in Latin as well as Arabic, and they support a confident portrait of a case-driven clinical empiricist. His philosophical and anti-religious works survive largely in fragments and in the polemics of opponents, so the radical thinker must be reconstructed from hostile or secondhand material. The two figures are joined by a single disposition toward reason, but the evidence for each stands on very different ground.

This asymmetry shapes every strong claim about him. To call al-Razi a freethinker who anticipated later rationalist critiques of religion is to lean on testimony, by writers such as the Ismaili theologians who attacked him, whose interest was to make his views look as scandalous as possible. To restrict him to the medicine is to set aside a body of report too substantial to dismiss entirely. The honest position holds both: a securely documented clinician and a philosophical provocateur whose precise commitments may not be fully recoverable.
advanced.worldOfIdeas:
Al-Razi's relationship to Galen is more interesting than the label rebel suggests. Al-Shukuk ala Jalinus, his Doubts about Galen, is not a rejection of the Galenic system but an internal critique that accepts much of its framework while challenging specific doctrines where, al-Razi argues, observation or consistency tells against the master. The work assumes that Galen can be wrong and that a later physician with good evidence may correct him. That assumption, rather than any single correction, is the radical move, since it treats the authoritative corpus as open to revision rather than as a closed deposit to be transmitted.

This empiricism connects to his most contested philosophical positions. In the reports of his metaphysics he is said to have held a doctrine of five eternal principles, including matter, time, and space, a scheme closer to certain Greek and Indian cosmologies than to Islamic creation theology. The same independence that let him doubt Galen apparently let him doubt the prophets. Whether his medical empiricism and his religious skepticism form a single coherent philosophy, or whether modern readers impose that unity on a scattered and partly hostile record, is a question the surviving sources cannot decisively settle.
advanced.centralIdea:
The case for al-Razi as a genuine clinical empiricist rests on the texture of his writing, not on a stated method. His monograph on smallpox and measles works by differential description: it isolates the features by which two superficially similar eruptive fevers can be told apart, and it does so through reported observation rather than deduction from humoral theory. The case histories embedded in the Hawi preserve particulars, including failures, of a kind that a purely doctrinal medicine would have no reason to record.

The interpretive danger is anachronism. It is tempting to read al-Razi as a proto-scientist testing hypotheses, but his framework remained broadly Galenic and humoral, and his alchemy mixed empirical procedure with assumptions no modern chemist would accept. The stronger reading is more precise: within an inherited theoretical system, al-Razi gave observation an unusually high evidential weight and was willing to let particular cases stand against general authority. That is a real and uncommon commitment, and it does not require turning him into a modern figure to be significant.
advanced.howSpread:
The Latin afterlife of al-Razi raises the same transmission problem that attends all the translated Arabic sciences: the Rhazes read in European universities is a text refracted through translators and through selective copying. The Continens was prized partly for its bulk, an encyclopedic reservoir of cases and citations, which meant it was mined rather than read whole, and excerpted in ways that could flatten his clinical voice into one authority among many. The Liber ad Almansorem, by contrast, circulated as a teaching unit, and its ninth book in particular became a fixture of the medical curriculum.

The contrast with Ibn Sina is instructive. The systematic, philosophically integrated Canon proved more teachable than al-Razi's looser, case-based corpus, and it progressively displaced him in formal instruction even as his clinical observations retained authority. This is not simply a story of one physician eclipsing another. It reflects a preference, in both Islamic and European medical education, for ordered system over accumulated case, a preference that arguably cost later medicine some of the observational openness al-Razi modeled.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
The hardest question about al-Razi is whether the radical freethinker of modern accounts is a historical figure or a reconstruction. The works in which he allegedly denied prophecy, attacked the major religions, and exalted reason above revelation do not survive as continuous texts. They are known mainly through refutation, above all from Ismaili writers such as Abu Hatim al-Razi and later Nasir-i Khusraw, who quoted him to condemn him. Hostile transmission is not worthless, but it systematically selects and sharpens the most provocative claims, and it offers no way to recover qualifications, contexts, or rhetorical purpose.

This creates a genuine scholarly fault line. One tradition, drawn to the boldness of the reported views, treats al-Razi as one of the most uncompromising rationalists of the premodern world. A more cautious position notes that almost everything striking attributed to him comes filtered through enemies, and that the actual al-Razi may have been less systematic and less categorical than either his admirers or his attackers suggest. The dispute cannot be resolved by finding a compromise reading of the polemics; it would require sources that, as far as is known, no longer exist. What remains defensible is that al-Razi unsettled his contemporaries enough to provoke sustained refutation, which is itself evidence that his challenge to religious authority was real, whatever its exact shape.

foundation.theStory: Al-Razi is a case study in how a premodern thinker can be securely known in one domain and only dimly known in another. As a physician he is exceptionally well documented: his major medical works survive, several reached Europe in Latin translation, and his clinical method can be read directly. As a philosopher and critic of religion he is known mainly through fragments and through the writings of opponents who set out to refute him. The same man is, depending on the subject, one of the best-attested and one of the most uncertain figures of his age.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern how far al-Razi's clinical empiricism should be read as anticipating later scientific method, and how much weight to place on the reports of his radical religious views. On the first, scholars differ over whether his emphasis on observation marks a real methodological departure or a strong tendency within an essentially Galenic framework. On the second, the difficulty is that his most provocative philosophical claims survive mostly through hostile sources, so any portrait of al-Razi the freethinker depends on how much one trusts writers who wanted to discredit him.
foundation.whyItMatters: Al-Razi connects the history of medicine, the transmission of Greek learning into Arabic and then Latin, the early history of chemistry, and the long argument over the relationship between reason and revealed religion. Understanding him is a way into how clinical observation developed before modern science, how medical authority was built and contested, and how we handle the recovery of ideas that survive only through their enemies.
questions: Al-Razi wrote a whole book pointing out where Galen seemed to be wrong, yet he kept using Galen's basic framework. What is the difference between correcting an authority on specific points and rejecting that authority altogether — and why might the first be the more powerful move?

Most of al-Razi's radical religious views come down to us through writers who quoted him in order to attack him. How should a careful reader weigh claims that survive only through hostile sources, and what would it take to trust them?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Lenn E. Goodman's studies of al-Razi's philosophy make the strongest case for taking his rationalism seriously as a coherent position, while Sarah Stroumsa, in work on medieval freethinkers, situates the reports of his religious skepticism within the polemics that preserved them and presses the question of their reliability. Paul Kraus's early collection and analysis of the philosophical fragments remains foundational for anyone working on the non-medical al-Razi. On the medical side, historians such as Peter E. Pormann and Emilie Savage-Smith place his clinical writing and his hospital practice within the wider development of medieval Islamic medicine.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence is strikingly uneven. The medical corpus is rich: the Hawi, the Mansuri, and the treatise on smallpox and measles survive, and the Latin reception is documented through manuscripts and university records. The philosophical and anti-religious works are mostly lost as continuous texts and reach us through fragments and through the refutations of opponents, especially Ismaili writers. This means claims about al-Razi the physician can be grounded directly in his words, while claims about al-Razi the freethinker rest heavily on inference and on adversarial testimony whose accuracy cannot be fully checked.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong account of al-Razi must hold the two figures apart before trying to join them. It can argue, on solid ground, that he gave clinical observation unusual evidential weight within a still-Galenic medicine, citing the differential method of the smallpox treatise and the case records of the Hawi. It must be more guarded about the philosophy: the defensible claim is not that al-Razi proved a systematic rejection of prophecy, but that he provoked enough refutation to show his challenge to religious authority was real, while conceding that its exact content is partly unrecoverable. The weakest move is to let the dramatic reported views stand as settled fact; the strongest is to specify precisely how much each part of the record can bear.
questions: Goodman reads al-Razi as a coherent rationalist philosopher; a more cautious reading treats the reports of his views as too fragmentary and too hostile to support that portrait. What kind of evidence would move you toward one position or the other, and why is that evidence so hard to obtain?

Al-Razi's case-based medicine was eventually overshadowed in formal teaching by Ibn Sina's more systematic Canon. What does it tell us about medical education that an ordered system proved more teachable than an accumulation of observed cases?

research.topic: The treatise on smallpox and measles and its clinical method
research.content: It is well established that al-Razi wrote a treatise, Kitab al-Judari wa al-Hasba, that distinguishes smallpox from measles through clinical description, and that this work was translated into Latin and other languages and circulated widely in Europe. The text survives, and its differential, observation-based approach to the two diseases is documented in the source itself and in a long line of later editions and translations. The treatise is routinely cited as one of the clearest examples of careful bedside observation in premodern medicine, independent of the larger debate over how far to read modern empiricism into his work.

research.topic: How far al-Razi's clinical empiricism prefigures later scientific method
research.content: There is active scholarly discussion about whether al-Razi's emphasis on observation and his case records represent a genuine methodological departure or a strong tendency within an essentially Galenic and humoral framework. Recent work in the history of medieval Islamic medicine has refined this question, examining his hospital practice, his use of case histories, and his critique of Galen in detail. The picture is being revised away from both the older image of an isolated proto-scientist and any dismissal of his originality, toward a more precise account of how much evidential weight he gave to particular cases within an inherited theory.

research.topic: The exact content of al-Razi's views on prophecy and religion
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: the complete, continuous texts of the works in which al-Razi is said to have criticized prophecy and organized religion, in his own words and with his own qualifications and arguments. Why unavailable: those works do not survive intact; they are known mainly through fragments and through the refutations of opponents, especially Ismaili theologians who quoted him in order to condemn him, and hostile transmission selects and sharpens the most provocative material. What can be responsibly inferred: that al-Razi prized reason highly, that he questioned religious authority enough to provoke sustained refutation, and that his stance unsettled contemporaries. What must remain unknowable: the precise scope, qualifications, and rhetorical purpose of his religious claims, and therefore whether the systematic freethinker of some modern accounts matches the historical al-Razi or is partly a reconstruction built from his enemies' framing.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Master Clinician
comparativeNarrative.content: Historians of medicine treat al-Razi above all as an observer. What matters in this view is the bedside. The case records, the hospital practice, and the treatise that told smallpox and measles apart by their signs rather than by theory. He used the Greek inheritance, especially Galen, but he was willing to record a case that broke the rules. He even wrote a whole book on where Galen seemed wrong. From this angle al-Razi matters less for any single doctrine than for a habit of mind. He insisted that what a doctor actually sees can correct what the authorities say.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Dangerous Rationalist
comparativeNarrative.content: Scholars of Islamic thought often stress a different al-Razi. This is the philosopher reported to have placed reason above revelation and to have questioned prophecy itself. Here he marks a limit. He shows how far rational inquiry could go within the medieval Islamic world. The trouble is that his most radical works survive mainly through the writers who attacked him. Among them were Ismaili theologians who quoted him in order to refute him. The figure that results is genuinely contested. Some read him as a bold freethinker. Others warn that almost everything striking about his religious views reaches us already framed by his enemies.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Practical Alchemist
comparativeNarrative.content: Historians of chemistry see al-Razi mainly through his laboratory. Much alchemy of his time was wrapped in secrecy and mystical symbols. Al-Razi wrote in a plainer, more practical way. He described apparatus, listed procedures, and sorted substances into groups. For this he is often placed among the figures who helped move alchemy toward an experimental craft. This view stresses how close his medicine and his chemistry were. Both rested on handling real materials. It also notes the limit. His framework still mixed careful observation with assumptions no modern chemist would share.

comparativeSummary: The same man looks different depending on which of his careers a tradition cares about most. To historians of medicine he is the master clinician whose great achievement was observation at the bedside. To historians of Islamic thought he is the dangerous rationalist, a figure known largely through the enemies who tried to refute him. To historians of chemistry he is the practical alchemist who treated the laboratory as plainly as he treated the sickbed. Holding the three together shows what links them, an unusual trust in reason and direct evidence over inherited authority, and also why he is so unevenly known: the physician is documented in his own words, while the philosopher must be reconstructed from fragments and hostile reports.

rabbitHole.label: The Islamic Golden Age
rabbitHole.reason: Al-Razi's career as a hospital director and prolific medical writer was a product of the Islamic Golden Age — the period of Abbasid patronage, urban learning, and translated Greek science that gave him his sources, his institutions, and his audience. He is one of the flowering's defining clinical figures.

rabbitHole.label: The Graeco-Arabic Translation Movement
rabbitHole.reason: The translation movement made Galen, Hippocrates, and Aristotle available in Arabic, giving al-Razi the corpus he built on and the Galenic medicine he was willing to challenge. His Doubts about Galen is unthinkable without the prior translation of Galen into Arabic that the movement produced.

rabbitHole.label: Ibn Sina
rabbitHole.reason: Ibn Sina was the later Persian physician whose systematic Canon of Medicine progressively overshadowed al-Razi's looser, case-based corpus in formal teaching, even as al-Razi's clinical observations kept their authority. The contrast between system and case runs through the history of both Islamic and European medical education.

rabbitHole.label: Aristotle
rabbitHole.reason: Aristotle was the towering philosophical authority of al-Razi's intellectual world, and al-Razi's willingness to depart from Greek authority — in medicine from Galen, in philosophy from received cosmology — engages the same problem of how far inherited Greek doctrine should bind later inquiry.

rabbitHole.label: The Abbasid Caliphate
rabbitHole.reason: Al-Razi worked in Baghdad, the Abbasid capital, and directed a hospital there; the caliphate's cities, patronage, and institutions were the milieu in which his clinical practice and his writing took shape.

rabbitHole.label: Al-Khwarizmi
rabbitHole.reason: Al-Khwarizmi was an earlier scholar of the same Abbasid scientific flowering whose work, like al-Razi's, reached Europe through twelfth-century Latin translation. Comparing them shows the range of what the period produced and the uneven way that work entered later memory.

rabbitHole.label: Islam
rabbitHole.reason: Al-Razi's reported criticism of prophecy and organized religion places him in direct tension with the religious tradition of his world, and the refutations that preserve his views came from within Islamic theology. His case bears on the long argument over the limits of rational inquiry inside that tradition.

rabbitHole.label: Ibn al-Haytham
rabbitHole.reason: Ibn al-Haytham, the later student of optics, is the other great exemplar of experimental, observation-driven method in the medieval Islamic sciences; reading him alongside al-Razi clarifies what empirical inquiry meant in this tradition across different fields.

reference.author: Goodman, Lenn E.
reference.title: Muhammad ibn Zakariyya' al-Razi (entry in the Encyclopaedia of Islam)
reference.annotation: The standard scholarly synthesis of al-Razi's life, medicine, and philosophy, and the clearest statement of the case for taking his rationalism seriously as a coherent position. Foundational for the entry's handling of how the physician and the philosopher relate.

reference.author: Stroumsa, Sarah
reference.title: Freethinkers of Medieval Islam: Ibn al-Rawandi, Abu Bakr al-Razi, and Their Impact on Islamic Thought
reference.annotation: The essential study of al-Razi as a religious freethinker, and crucially of how his views survive through the polemics that preserved them. Drives the entry's repeated caution that the radical al-Razi is reconstructed from hostile and fragmentary sources.

reference.author: Pormann, Peter E.; Savage-Smith, Emilie
reference.title: Medieval Islamic Medicine
reference.annotation: The standard overview of medieval Islamic medicine, situating al-Razi's clinical writing, hospital practice, and case-based method within the wider tradition. The anchor for the entry's claims about his medicine and its institutional setting.

reference.author: Kraus, Paul
reference.title: Rasa'il falsafiyya (Abi Bakr Muhammadi filii Zachariae Raghensis opera philosophica)
reference.annotation: Kraus's collection of the surviving philosophical fragments is the foundational source for any work on the non-medical al-Razi, gathering the scattered material on which all later reconstructions of his philosophy depend.

reference.author: al-Razi, Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn Zakariya
reference.title: A Treatise on the Smallpox and Measles (Kitab al-Judari wa al-Hasba), trans. William Alexander Greenhill
reference.annotation: Greenhill's English translation of al-Razi's most famous clinical work gives direct access to the differential, observation-based method the entry treats as the core of his empiricism. The primary text behind the smallpox-and-measles thread.

reference.author: al-Razi, Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn Zakariya
reference.title: Kitab al-Hawi fi al-Tibb (The Comprehensive Book on Medicine)
reference.annotation: Al-Razi's vast medical compendium, assembled partly from his own case notes and Latinized as the Continens. The primary source for the case-based texture of his medicine and for the bulk that made the Latin reception possible.

commerce.title: Medieval Islamic Medicine
commerce.author: Peter E. Pormann and Emilie Savage-Smith
commerce.note: The best first book if you want al-Razi placed in his real world before tackling his own texts. It explains how hospitals, case records, and the Greek inheritance actually worked, in clear prose for readers new to the subject.

commerce.title: The House of Wisdom: How the Arabs Transformed Western Civilization
commerce.author: Jonathan Lyons
commerce.note: Read this for the larger story of Abbasid learning and its passage to Europe. A general-reader account of the translation movement and scientific culture that produced figures like al-Razi, useful before any technical study.

commerce.title: Freethinkers of Medieval Islam: Ibn al-Rawandi, Abu Bakr al-Razi, and Their Impact on Islamic Thought
commerce.author: Sarah Stroumsa
commerce.note: Turn here once you want to engage the controversial al-Razi seriously. Stroumsa is demanding but readable, and she is careful about exactly how reliable the sources for his religious views are — the heart of the debate over his legacy.

commerce.title: A Treatise on the Smallpox and Measles
commerce.author: Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi (trans. W. A. Greenhill)
commerce.note: Reach for this when you want to read al-Razi directly rather than through summary. Short, and the clearest window onto his bedside method, though the nineteenth-century translation reads as a period piece.

commerce.title: Islamic Medicine
commerce.author: Manfred Ullmann
commerce.note: For the reader ready for the specialist literature on medieval Islamic medicine and its sources. Concise but dense, the kind of reference that maps the whole field, including where al-Razi's texts and reception sit within it.

popularCulture.title: Rhazes in the history of medicine
popularCulture.description: In many popular histories, al-Razi appears under his Latin name, Rhazes, as the first doctor to tell smallpox and measles apart. The story is true, and it is a real achievement. But it often arrives as a single fact, stripped of the daily bedside work and the hospital records that made such a distinction possible in the first place.

popularCulture.title: The doctor who doubted Galen
popularCulture.description: Al-Razi is often remembered as the man who dared to challenge the great Greek authority Galen. The image is appealing and partly fair. He did write a book on where Galen seemed wrong. But the popular version can make him sound like a modern rebel, when in fact he still used most of Galen's system and corrected it from the inside.

popularCulture.title: Al-Razi as freethinker
popularCulture.description: Some modern writers celebrate al-Razi as an early freethinker who questioned prophets and organized religion a thousand years ago. There is something to this. But most of what we know about those views comes from enemies who quoted him to attack him. The bold, tidy rebel of popular accounts may be sharper and simpler than the real man was.

popularCulture.title: Al-Razi in the history of chemistry
popularCulture.description: Popular accounts of early chemistry often name al-Razi as a bridge from mystical alchemy to real laboratory work. He did write plainly about equipment and procedures, which was unusual for his time. The neat story of a leap from magic to science is too clean, though. His own work still mixed careful observation with ideas no chemist would accept today.

popularCulture.title: Hospitals named for al-Razi
popularCulture.description: Hospitals and medical buildings across Iran and the wider region carry al-Razi's name today, and his image appears on stamps and in textbooks as a national and Islamic scientific hero. This public memory keeps his fame alive. It also tends to smooth over the controversial thinker, presenting the celebrated physician while quietly setting aside the man who unsettled the religious authorities of his day.
